risks: R-1 Scope-consuming-product: KERNEL-004 is the largest WP in project history and risks the harness-consumes-product failure mode the reset brief escaped.

R-2 PRODUCTION-tier research-frontier risk: MoD and Subquadratic architectures may have no PRODUCTION engine path in 2026, while operator binding requires PRODUCTION tier for all 9 inference techniques.
R-3 Sandbox-architecture-flip-orphans-KERNEL-003: A non-Docker sandbox pick from the landscape scan could strand KERNEL-003 Docker integration code.
R-4 Spec-enrichment-becomes-its-own-project: The v02.186 enrichment is wide and could splinter into incremental drift bumps if not bounded.
R-5 LM-Studio/Ollama-lockout: The no-third-party-wrapping invariant could lock out the easiest local-model onboarding path for the operator.
R-6 Visual-debugger-parallel-mutation-path: A debugger built outside KernelActionCatalogV1/WriteBoxV1 would become a second authority-bypass write path.
R-7 Self-improvement-overfit: A self-improvement loop targeted at one measurable check will overfit to that check unless a held-out corpus guards promotion.
minimum_controls: Hard scope limit at the 30-stub-fold boundary; refinement explicitly forbids adding scope outside the folded stubs (R-1).
Research workstream 3 must resolve MoD/Subquadratic engine-path feasibility before signature; if no PRODUCTION path exists, escalate B-MoD-SUBQUAD-FEASIBILITY for operator scope decision (R-2).
Preserve KERNEL-003 sandbox lane as an adapter under the chosen sandbox architecture with explicit migration MTs (R-3).
Spec enrichment v02.186 is a single coherent bundle bump per CX-105C; no incremental enrichment drift (R-4).

Allow ExternalEngineImport as a non-authoritative pass-through lane so operator can point at a model file or local OpenAI-compatible endpoint without depending on LM Studio or Ollama as runtime authority (R-5).
All backend manipulation paths route through KernelActionCatalogV1 and WriteBoxV1; no debug-only direct DB writes (R-6).
Self-improvement target is a fixed test corpus; promotion requires operator review; loop-counter HBR-SWARM-002 caps runaway; metric must be validated against a held-out corpus before promotion (R-7).
Packet contract must reference this refinement contract before downstream launch (per KERNEL-003 control pattern).
Unsupported prose-only refinement claims become explicit migration debt per GLOBAL-SOT-018.

refinement.summary_criteria_note: These 16 operator-readable summaries complement (do not replace) the structured AC-* entries in `acceptance_criteria` below. Original field name `acceptance_criteria` (16 strings) was renamed `summary_criteria` 2026-05-18 when the spec v02.186 enrichment introduced the 52 AC-* IDs structured below.

acceptance_criteria.text: HANDSHAKE_BUILD_RULES.json at .GOV/roles_shared/records/HANDSHAKE_BUILD_RULES.json is the single source-of-truth for HBR-IDs across INT/SWARM/VIS/QUIET/MAN families (23 active rules at v1.1.0); any prose mention of an HBR-ID in spec, refinement, or work-packet documents resolves against this file.

HBR gate evaluation HARD runs at two enforcement points: (a) build time inside `just gov-check` via the `hbr-matrix-check.mjs` sub-check, and (b) handoff time on every governed inter-role transition (refinement->coder, coder->WP_VALIDATOR, WP_VALIDATOR->INTEGRATION_VALIDATOR, INTEGRATION_VALIDATOR->ORCHESTRATOR), emitting an `HBR_HANDOFF_GATE` event; a failed rule blocks the handoff.

HBR violations MUST emit typed `HBR_VIOLATION` receipts per the CX-130 schema with fields `hbr_id`, `wp_id`, `mt_id`, `role`, `evaluation_point` (build|handoff), `evidence_pointer`, `violation_class`; free-form prose is never the wire contract (may accompany as `notes` only).

Primitive `ProcessOwnershipLedger` is backed by the Postgres table `kernel_process_lifecycle` with columns `process_uuid` (UUID v7 per HBR-INT-008), `parent_session_id`, `sandbox_adapter_id`, `engine_kind` enum, `started_at`, `stopped_at`, `exit_code`, `model_artifact_sha256`, `work_profile_id`, `owner_role`, `owner_wp`.

HARD scope: every process spawned by Handshake (model engine, mechanical job, ASR worker, ComfyUI worker, sandbox container, plugin process, helper subprocess) MUST write START + STOP rows; NO exceptions; any spawn path that bypasses the ledger is a spec defect.

Ledger write path MUST be perf-safe: batched async writes via a bounded mpsc channel (spawn thread never blocks on Postgres), capped at N writes/sec configurable per Work Profile, and `FR-EVT-LEDGER-OVERFLOW` receipt + Diagnostics-panel degraded-mode flag on channel saturation (never silent drop).

Reclaim hooks fire on session close, failure, staleness, and operator cancel (per CX-503D + CX-212G); on fire they query `kernel_process_lifecycle` for the session's non-stopped rows, call `SandboxAdapter.kill` on each, and write STOP rows with `exit_code = -1`.

Visual debugger is Playwright driving Chrome DevTools Protocol against the WebView2 instance, launched with `WEBVIEW2_ADDITIONAL_BROWSER_ARGUMENTS=--remote-debugging-port=<random>` and per-test `WEBVIEW2_USER_DATA_FOLDER`; never modifies Handshake UI state outside test scope.

Visual debugger exposes Tauri IPC `kernel.visual_debug.screenshot(scope,opts)` (PNG via CDP Page.captureScreenshot), `kernel.visual_debug.dom_snapshot(scope)` (DOM JSON), and `kernel.visual_debug.console_stream(scope)` (renderer console + exception via Runtime.consoleAPICalled + Runtime.exceptionThrown + Playwright pageerror).

Capture matrix follows Playwright projects-matrix shape; each scenario produces a manifest entry with `scenario_id`, `route`, `viewport`, `color_scheme`, `locale`, `edge_state_tag`, `wait_for`, `mask_selectors`, `baseline_hash`; baselines content-addressed under `.GOV/visual_baselines/`; drift triggers a typed visual-regression receipt.

Backend inspector plane MUST bind to `127.0.0.1:<random>` only (never `0.0.0.0`), use a per-launch random port discovered via Diagnostics panel or `kernel.inspector.port()`, and be feature-gated behind `cfg(feature = "inspector")` so it is NOT compiled into release builds.

`InspectorReadV1` Rust trait has NO `&mut self` methods and lives in the `inspector_read` crate which does NOT depend on the write-side crate; a workspace-level deny-lint blocks any reverse dependency edge so mutation cannot leak into the inspector path at compile time.

Inspector plane mutations route exclusively through `KernelActionCatalogV1` via the single `/inspector/v1/replay-drive` endpoint which accepts ONLY a catalog action id plus a signed `WriteBoxV1` envelope; any other body returns 403; the inspector never has a parallel mutation path.

For any window opened during automated GUI test runs or governed agent activity, the Tauri WindowBuilder MUST set `.visible(false).focus(false).focusable(false).skip_taskbar(true).always_on_bottom(true).decorations(false)`; any inverted flag is an HBR-QUIET-001 violation.

Focus-audit subsystem uses `SetWinEventHook(EVENT_SYSTEM_FOREGROUND, ..., WINEVENT_OUTOFCONTEXT | WINEVENT_SKIPOWNPROCESS)` via the `wineventhook` Rust crate to log every foreground transition `(timestamp, hwnd, pid, exe_name)` keyed by `run_id`; harness assertion: no foreground change resolves to Handshake's pid or to any process spawned by Handshake per ProcessOwnershipLedger.

Keyboard-injection negative test installs a `WH_KEYBOARD_LL` low-level hook checking `KBDLLHOOKSTRUCT.flags & LLKHF_INJECTED`, fires `SendInput` shortcut sequences while a hidden Tauri window is alive, and asserts (a) no `#[tauri::command]` handler fired, (b) no state mutation occurred, (c) the LL-hook saw the injection.

Workspace clippy lint rule fails the build on any reference to `set_focus`, `show` (on hidden windows), `unminimize`, `AllowSetForegroundWindow`, `AttachThreadInput`, or `SetForegroundWindow` (from non-foreground processes) outside `operator_foreground::*`; `LockSetForegroundWindow` is never used.

Foreground exception (HBR-QUIET-004) is opt-in per packet: a WP needing OS-foreground interaction MUST declare `packet.requires_foreground = true` before any run, use a bounded controlled test window with timeout + auto-dismiss, and surface an operator-visible warning (Tauri notification + Diagnostics-panel banner) before launch.

Primitive `kernel_swarm_test_harness` spawns N concurrent governed sessions against the same Handshake instance with no shared session state between concurrent sessions (each session independent and re-entrant).
Perf scaling validated to N=8 minimum per HBR-SWARM-001/002/003/004; N is parameterized and higher values are supported but not required by v02.186 acceptance.

Harness surfaces lock/lease behavior under contention, cancellation propagation when sessions terminate mid-mutation, loop-counter behavior (HBR-SWARM-002) when a session loops on a stuck precondition, and ProcessOwnershipLedger consistency when multiple sessions write concurrently; failures here gate KERNEL-004 acceptance.

`SandboxAdapter` Rust trait lives in `src/backend/handshake_core/src/sandbox/mod.rs` and exposes `spawn`, `exec`, `fs_bind`, `net_policy`, `kill`, `status`, `exit_code`, `capabilities`; trait surface MUST NOT expose adapter-specific types (callers reason only in `ProcessSpec` / `ProcessHandle` / `AdapterCapabilities`).

KERNEL-004 ships three adapter implementations: `WSL2PodmanAdapter` (DEFAULT; rootless Podman in WSL2; bind-mount large GGUF; near-native CUDA), `WindowsNativeJailAdapter` (Win32 Job Objects + AppContainer + Restricted Tokens, wrapping `codex-windows-sandbox`/`rappct` Rust crate; never hand-rolled), `DockerAdapter` (compat-only-not-default; preserves KERNEL-003 DocketAdapter).

Each adapter publishes a machine-readable `AdapterCapabilities` struct readable via Tauri IPC with fields `filesystem_isolation_strength`, `network_isolation_strength`, `gpu_passthrough`, `stdio_throughput_class`, `win32_native_fidelity`, `cross_machine_portable`; consumed by ModelRuntime (§4.6) and ProcessOwnershipLedger (§5.7).

Every model process Handshake spawns MUST be (1) a child of a `SandboxAdapter` (§3.5) with no bare `std::process::Command` model spawns, and (2) registered with START + STOP rows in `ProcessOwnershipLedger` (§5.7) via the perf-mitigated batched/async/capped write path; violations fail the §5.6 HBR gate.

HARD: no Ollama daemon, no LM Studio process, no third-party model-server wrapper is an authoritative ModelRuntime instance under KERNEL-004 and onward; §4.6 ModelRuntime + LocalModelAdapter is the normative path and §4.2.4 is rewritten in v02.186 to remove the Ollama-as-primary recommendation.

Operator MAY point Handshake at a model file path on disk or at a local OpenAI-compatible HTTP endpoint via the `ExternalEngineImport` lane for compatibility; such pointers are NOT authoritative ModelRuntime instances (Handshake does not manage their lifecycle, endpoint is never written to ProcessOwnershipLedger as Handshake-owned, model is flagged `provider="external_compat"`), but LlmClient (CX-101) still mediates every call.

`ModelRuntime` Rust trait exposes `load`, `unload`, `generate` (streaming `TokenStream`), `score`, `embed`, `capabilities`, `kv_cache`, `lora_stack`, `steering_hooks`, `cancel`; trait surface MUST be engine-agnostic (callers never see `llama_cpp_2::*` or `candle_core::*` types).
`LlamaCppRuntime` (DEFAULT) wraps `llama-cpp-2` Rust crate; covers ~all GGUF transformer models; required path for KV-cache + KV-quant + self-speculative decoding (Section 4.7.1g).

`CandleRuntime` wraps `candle-core` + `candle-transformers`; required path for hook-requiring techniques (activation steering §4.7.1c, refusal vector §4.7.1d, CAA §4.7.1e) and for subquadratic architectures (§4.7.1h: Mamba2, RWKV v5-v7); a model is bound to exactly one adapter at register time (re-binding requires unload + re-register).

Per-model `ModelCapabilities` declares `supports_lora`, `supports_kv_prefix_cache`, `supports_kv_quantization` (none|q4|q8|q4_q8_mix), `supports_activation_steering`, `supports_subquadratic`, `supports_speculative_draft`, `supports_eagle3`; consumed by frontend via `kernel.model_runtime.capabilities(model_id)` to drive Inference Lab UI toggle visibility (hidden, not greyed).

Eight production techniques ship at GA in v02.186 with declared runtime adapter, Work Profile knob (`settings.exec_policy` extension), observability event family (FR-EVT-LLM-INFER-*), and conservative-default opt-in flag: (a) LoRA hot-swap, (b) KV caching, (c) Activation Steering, (d) Refusal Vector, (e) CAA, (f) Abliteration (offline tool), (g) Self-Speculative Decoding (ngram|draft|eagle3-when-supported), (h) Subquadratic with full feature parity (LoRA + steering + state-vector persistence + cross-session restore).

Mixture-of-Depths (Raposo et al. 2024) is spec-deferred to Phase 3 roadmap; KERNEL-004 ships preliminary research only (§6.8); explicit non-actions: NO new work-packet stub for MoD, NO new WP created for MoD, NO runtime adapter capability for MoD in v02.186.

Abliteration is OFFLINE TOOL ONLY: never inserted into a hot inference path / `ModelRuntime.generate`; output is a new derived model artifact (own ModelId + on-disk GGUF/safetensors), passes through §4.8 content-review pipeline before any Skill Bank reference (PII scan + license tagging + provenance); ProcessOwnershipLedger records the job as `engine_kind="abliteration_tool"`.

MoD preliminary research subsection (§6.8) captures: Raposo et al. 2024 (arXiv:2404.02258 DeepMind) context, reference implementations (`sramshetty/mixture-of-depths` unofficial training-focused; `raymin0223/mixture_of_recursions` NeurIPS 2025), comparison vs shipped techniques (layer-skip §4.5; self-speculative §4.7.1g), and five open research questions (training cost, CandleRuntime hook gaps, capability declaration shape, inference perf budget, interaction with KV-cache prefix sharing).

Explicit non-implementation invariant: NO implementation in KERNEL-004, NO new stub created for MoD (work-packet stub, refinement, microtask plan -- none), NO new WP created for MoD; Phase 3 roadmap reflection bullet tracks the deferral; revisit when (a) a production engine path materializes, or (b) operator explicitly authorizes custom training+runtime work.

Distillation is default OFF for every governed session; opt-in is per-session (never persistent default) via operator setting `DISTILL_CORPUS=true` at session close (post-hoc); scope of opt-in is a single session's events + artifacts and does not extend transitively.

Before any distillation candidate enters the Skill Bank it MUST pass: PII scan (regex + NER for names/emails/phones/credentials/machine ids; failures emit `FR-EVT-DISTILL-PII-DETECT` and block promotion), license tagging (provenance for third-party fragments; untaggable fragments quarantined), and dedup (content-hash + embedding-similarity near-dup); pipeline re-uses §9 Skill Bank schema (no new schema in v02.186).

Memory V0+ self-improvement loop targets validator first-pass PASS rate on a fixed ~30-item HBR test-packet corpus with HARD safeguards: 60/20/20 train/dev/holdout split with holdout encrypted-at-rest, multi-metric promotion floor (dev PASS up AND latency p95 not regressed AND capsule bytes not regressed AND holdout PASS rate not regressed), Goodhart sentinel (auto-pause if dev-vs-holdout gap widens monotonically 3 iterations), HBR-SWARM-002 loop counter cap with `FR-EVT-DISTILL-LOOP-CAP` receipt, and PromotionGate operator review per promotion.

Editable surface for V0 loop: ModelManual capsule text (§10.15) and retrieval policy parameters (top-k, capsule budget bytes); explicit non-goals (NOT editable in V0): spec text (shadow-authority risk), system prompts shared across roles (blast radius too wide), LoRA weights (no training infra in V0).

ModelManual authority lives at `src/backend/handshake_core/src/model_manual/mod.rs` (CX-200 BACKEND root) as typed Rust structs (e.g., `ManualCommand`, `ManualWorkflow`, `ManualSafetyConstraint`); there is no separate `MODEL_MANUAL.md` file that competes for authority.

stub_fold_preservation_map.preserved_intent:
- What: Implement the generate-capture-compare-fix visual debugging loop for GUI WPs. After a coder commits GUI changes, the post-commit hook captures a screenshot, compares against the design baseline or previous screenshot, identifies visual discrepancies, and sends the visual diff to the validator alongside the code diff. The validator reviews both code and visual output. If visual mismatch exceeds threshold, the coder receives STEER with visual regression details.
- Why: Based on Kimi K2.5 visual debugging loop pattern. Code diffs alone cannot reveal visual regressions. Governed coders working on GUI WPs need automated visual feedback to catch layout breaks, styling mismatches, and rendering issues that pass code review but fail visual inspection. This loop closes the gap between code correctness and visual correctness.

- What: Make cross-tool interaction mechanically consistent across Handshake's mechanical and AI tool surfaces per the spec 6.0.1 invariants (no shadow pipelines; artifact-first I/O; capability-gated side effects; Flight Recorder evidence; Operator Console inspectability). | - Why: Without one shared interaction pipeline, each tool family invents its own logging, caching, capability enforcement, and provenance plumbing and the system becomes un-debuggable and unsafe.

- What: Implement the session spawn lifecycle (SessionSpawnRequest/Response), enforce hard depth + active-children caps, and provide announce-back via Role Mailbox with summary artifacts.
- Why: Prevent runaway delegation storms and make sub-session work auditable, bounded, and safely mergeable back into primary flows.

- What: Enforce workspace safety boundaries for parallel sessions: worktree isolation and/or strict file-scope locks, deny-by-default cross-session access, command denylist, and merge-back discipline.
- Why: Parallel sessions touching the same repo/workspace can silently overwrite or conflict without deterministic isolation and fail-closed execution rules.

- What: Enforce session-scoped capability intersection and consent gating for cloud calls across parallel sessions, and implement inbound trust boundary rules for cross-session/system message provenance.
- Why: Parallel orchestration is unsafe without deny-by-default per-session effective capabilities, durable consent receipts for fan-out, and strict inbound provenance/anti-bypass constraints.

- What: Establish a reproducible PostgreSQL developer/test container matrix for the PostgreSQL-primary pivot, including local startup, migration reset, seeded fixtures, and CI-ready smoke profiles.
- Why: Follow-on PostgreSQL control-plane packets need a reliable database target under high script load; otherwise each WP re-discovers container setup, connection strings, migration order, and PostgreSQL failure modes.

- What: Implement a deterministic, auditable LLM provider registry and at least one cloud-capable
  provider adapter (OpenAI-compatible HTTP API), integrated with Work Profiles, ModelSwap, and
  cloud consent enforcement.
- Why: Product must be project/task agnostic and portable across environments, while preserving
  strict safety (cloud leakage guard + explicit consent) and strong observability.

- What: Implement the ModelSwapRequest protocol and sequential model swaps, including state persistence and ACE recompile before resuming execution.
- Why: Phase 1 runtime integration requires deterministic model resource management and auditable swap events.

- What: Define and implement deterministic "Context Packs" used to onboard any model instance to a
  role + task, including on swap/escalation, with strict provenance, hashing, and auditability.
- Why: Without deterministic onboarding artifacts, multi-model execution becomes non-reproducible
  and governance enforcement drifts per provider/model.

- What: Introduce ModelSession as the persisted unit of multi-turn orchestration and implement the Phase 1 Session Scheduler baseline (`model_run` job_kind) with queueing, cancellation, and concurrency limits.
- Why: Without a first-class session data model + scheduler, parallel and multi-turn execution becomes completion-only, non-auditable, and unsafe to steer/recover.

- What: Implement Phase 1 foundations for governed multi-model execution (DOCS_ONLY vs AI_ENABLED), strict file-scope locks across concurrent WPs/MTs, and compact lifecycle telemetry (HSK_STATUS + CX-MM codes) with Flight Recorder correlation.
- Why: Enable safe parallelism and deterministic recovery without governance bypass, conflicting edits, or operator confusion.

- What: Make LoRA selection actually affect the provider request path by adding an optional lora_id field and plumbing it end-to-end.
- Why: Current code tracks lora_id in state but the LLM request has no LoRA field, making selection a no-op and violating spec intent.

- What: complete the late-stage Skill Bank / distillation backend so teacher-student lineage, candidate selection, checkpoint/eval gating, adapter-only training posture, and rollback-safe promotion become explicit runtime contracts.
- Why: the spec now hardcodes LoRA / QLoRA / DoRA posture, PromptEnvelope / Context Pack reuse, and distillation evidence requirements, but the implementation path still needs a dedicated packet.

- What: Ship Phase 1 dynamic-compute foundations: accept `settings.exec_policy` in the runtime contract, govern per-role compute presets (standard / fast_exact / fast_approx) with waiver-required approximation, and emit Flight Recorder telemetry for requested vs effective policy.
- Why: Enable future experimentation without silent quality regressions or audit gaps; keep default posture "standard exact".

- What: Add bi-temporal timestamps to MemoryItem — `valid_from`/`valid_until` (when the fact was true in the world) and `recorded_at`/`invalidated_at` (when we recorded/invalidated it). Ports the Graphiti/Zep temporal knowledge graph pattern.
- Why: FEMS has `created_at` and `last_verified_at` but cannot answer "what did the model know at time T?" Replay mode (FEMS-EVAL-001.4) requires reconstructing the exact MemoryPack from a past session — which means knowing which memories existed AND were considered valid at that moment. Without bi-temporal indexing, replay can't distinguish between "memory existed but was superseded" and "memory didn't exist yet".

- What: After a MemoryPack is used in a model call, track the outcome and feed it back into memory scoring. Good outcomes (MT completed, validator PASS) boost the items in that pack. Bad outcomes (MT failed, escalation) penalize them. Over time the scoring formula self-tunes. Ports the Cognee self-improving memory pattern.
- Why: The injection scoring formula uses static weights (importance, recency, trust). Without outcome feedback, a memory that consistently leads to failures keeps getting injected with the same score. The feedback loop creates a reinforcement signal: memories that help stay, memories that mislead fade.

- What: Implement the deterministic scoring formula for MemoryPack item ranking and a progressive retrieval pipeline that degrades gracefully under CPU/GPU load instead of failing hard latency gates. Ports the injection scoring formula from repo governance and applies the progressive retrieval pattern from the memory systems research.
- Why: FEMS spec says "drop lowest priority items" on budget overflow but defines no scoring algorithm. The p95 500ms latency target is unrealistic under heavy load (parallel local models, browser, other apps). Handshake needs tiered degradation — skip expensive retrieval tiers under load, build MemoryPack from whatever tier completes, log the degradation.

- What: Add a `pinned: true` flag to MemoryItem that guarantees inclusion in every MemoryPack before the scoring formula runs on the remaining budget. Ports the Letta/MemGPT "core memory" pattern — items always present in context like RAM.
- Why: Some memories should ALWAYS reach the model: user preferences, safety constraints, project identity, critical procedural rules. Currently MemoryPack is compiled entirely via retrieval scoring, so even critical items can be displaced by high-recency low-importance items. The spec allows procedural items in stable prefix if trusted and review-approved — pinning formalizes this into a first-class mechanism.

- What: Implement a scheduled FEMS hygiene job that runs consolidation, pruning, flagging, and promotion using a cost-effective model. Ports the battle-tested Memory Manager pattern from repo governance into the product Execution / Job Runtime.
- Why: FEMS defines `memory_consolidate_v0.1` and `memory_forget_v0.1` job kinds but has no scheduled orchestration. Without active hygiene, LongTermMemory grows unbounded, stale items pollute MemoryPack injection, and contradictions accumulate unresolved.

- What: Add operational health calibration signals to the DCC Front End Memory Panel so the operator can see whether FEMS is healthy or degrading. Ports the calibration signals pattern from repo governance memory hygiene reports.
- Why: The DCC Memory Panel (§10.11.5.14) specifies Memory Browser, Write Review, MemoryPack Preview, and Conflict queue — but no health dashboard. Without calibration signals, memory degradation (bloat, stale dominance, trust drift, embedding gaps) is invisible until it manifests as poor model behavior.

- What: Define and enforce deterministic acceptance/validation for FEMS Phase 1 delivery.
- Why: Phase 1 explicitly requires reproducible memory pack behavior and visible operator controls before the feature can be considered done.

- What: Define and later implement one governed retrieval-mode policy for Handshake so `QueryPlan`, `RetrievalTrace`, Project Brain, Prompt-to-Spec Router, Loom-aware graph expansion, Work Packet loads, and Micro-Task context assembly choose the cheapest authoritative retrieval path instead of defaulting to hybrid retrieval.
- Why: Handshake now has rich retrieval substrates and graph signals, but it still needs explicit no-RAG and lower-cost retrieval rules so exact identifiers, authoritative packet state, bounded local-small-model loops, and freshness-sensitive operations do not get blurred into one generic semantic-search path.

- What: Provide a dedicated exporter that takes a `trace_id` (retrieval execution) and emits a redacted-by-default bundle containing QueryPlan/RetrievalTrace + budgets/cache markers + selected spans and referenced artifacts.
- Why: Make retrieval issues debuggable, explainable, and shareable with deterministic evidence without leaking sensitive payloads.

- What: Feed spawn tree conversation histories into the distillation pipeline so child session summaries become training data for parent-role specialization and LoRA fine-tuning.
- Why: A spawn tree represents a structured decomposition of complex work. The parent-child conversation trail is high-quality training signal for model specialization — the parent asked a question, the child solved it. This is natural teacher-student data for distillation.

- What: Implement Role Mailbox threads/messages with transcription links and always-on deterministic repo export to `.GOV/ROLE_MAILBOX/`.
- Why: Enable LLM role-to-role coordination without Operator copy/paste while preserving "chat is not state" and auditable governance.

- What: Define and later implement the durable Role Mailbox executor-routing, claim or lease, takeover, and response-authority contract across Role Mailbox, Dev Command Center, Locus Work Tracking, Micro-Task Executor, Task Board, and Work Packet follow-up views.
- Why: Handshake now has typed mailbox messages, verifier-loop control, and queue-remediation posture, but it still needs one lawful ownership layer so parallel local models, cloud models, reviewers, validators, operators, and workflow automation do not race to answer the same thread or silently seize work.

- What: Define and later implement the bounded mailbox-loop-control contract that lets Role Mailbox coordinate Micro-Task retries, verifier feedback, verification-needed waits, escalation, and completion reports through structured checkpoints instead of transcript replay.
- Why: Handshake now has typed Role Mailbox threads and portable workflow law, but it still needs one durable loop-control contract so small local models, cloud models, reviewers, and operators can resume or inspect Micro-Task loops from compact state rather than chat chronology.

- What: Make micro-task iteration a first-class, auditable job loop with deterministic escalation and hard-gate pauses (auto-generation of MT definitions from WP scope, fresh-context-per-iteration execution, completion-signal parsing with anti-gaming, bounded iteration limits), wire MT validation through the Mechanical Tool Bus (PlannedOperation envelope, capability checks, FR-EVT-MT-012 emission), persist MT state with ProgressArtifact + RunLedger atomic writes and crash recovery, support a 6-level escalation chain (7B->7B-alt->13B->13B-alt->32B->HARD_GATE) with LoRA selection by task_tags, and emit DistillationCandidate artifacts + FR-EVT-MT-015 for logging-only distillation in Phase 1. | - Why: Without a first-class MT executor loop, MT iteration becomes implicit chat replay, escalation is non-deterministic, distillation evidence is lost, and silent infinite loops become possible.

- What: Add checkpointing and idempotent recovery/resume for interrupted `model_run` jobs and sessions, with deterministic failure modes and explicit evidence.
- Why: Session state loss on crash is explicitly listed as an incident-predicting anti-pattern; without checkpointing, multi-session orchestration becomes fragile and unsafe.

- What: Implement session-scoped observability for multi-session execution: new FR event families, base schema correlation via model_session_id, and span bindings (ModelSessionSpan + ActivitySpan).
- Why: Multi-session orchestration without strong observability becomes un-debuggable and unsafe; the spec requires session-wide queries to work via model_session_id even without spans.
